text: Refactoring means improving the structure of existing code without changing its external behavior. The goal is not to add new features, but to make the code cleaner and easier to understand. For example, you might rename unclear variables, break long functions into smaller ones, or remove duplicate logic. This process helps reduce "technical debt," which is the implied cost of taking shortcuts or writing messy code. By regularly refactoring, you improve readability and maintainability, so future changes become simpler and less risky. Many developers use automated tests to check that behavior stays the same during refactoring. A clean codebase saves time and effort in the long run.
